behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract
behaviors.justification: Clause extraction quality depends entirely on the real LLM's semantic understanding of legal language. A unit test with mocked extraction results would always pass, hiding real model failure modes like missing subtle obligations or dates hidden in conditional clauses. The real failure—'the LLM misses key terms in real contracts'—can only be caught by running the actual model on real contracts with verified ground truth.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor on 10+ real legal contracts with documented key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties). For each contract, verify: (1) all documented key terms are identified by the model, (2) no documented key terms are missed, (3) false positives are minimized. Test across domains (employment, vendor, lease, commercial) and complexity levels to ensure robust extraction under model non-determinism.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic
behaviors.justification: Detecting euphemistic or indirect risk language requires the real LLM's semantic understanding. Mocking the risk assessor would hide whether the actual model can detect subtle phrasing—e.g., 'unfavorable market conditions' as a euphemism for 'contract may be broken.' The real failure is semantic-level: the model fails to flag language that the policy should catch. This is only catchable with the actual model and realistic risk-policy configurations.
behaviors.test_description: Run the risk assessor on 10+ contracts with both direct risk language ('automatic termination', 'unlimited liability') and indirect/euphemistic language ('market conditions change', 'adjustment mechanism'). For each configured risk policy (conservative, moderate, permissive), verify: (1) direct problematic language is flagged, (2) indirect language matching the policy threshold is flagged, (3) safe language passes without flags. Validate against human-reviewed ground truth to ensure policy application is correct.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction
behaviors.justification: Compliance determination depends on the real LLM's knowledge of jurisdiction-specific regulations and ability to map contract language to regulatory requirements. Mocking compliance results would hide whether the model actually understands the regulations—e.g., whether it knows what GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA require, or whether it misses a subtle compliance gap. The real failure is domain knowledge: the model doesn't know or misapplies the regulations. Only the actual model can be tested here.
behaviors.test_description: Run the compliance checker on 10+ contracts with pre-determined compliance status across 3–5 distinct jurisdictions (e.g., EU GDPR, US HIPAA, California). For each contract, verify: (1) clearly compliant contracts are correctly flagged as compliant, (2) clearly non-compliant contracts are correctly flagged as non-compliant, (3) edge cases near regulatory boundaries are handled correctly (not always approved or always rejected). Use contracts reviewed by legal experts with ground truth compliance labels per jurisdiction.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says "standard" but risk assessor says "high risk"), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution
behaviors.justification: Although merge is deterministic business logic, testing it requires real reviewer outputs. A mock-based Integration test could verify merge handles artificial conflicts, but would miss real failures: (1) real reviewers produce conflicts in formats the merge code doesn't expect, (2) natural conflicts occur in specific clause types that mocks don't generate, (3) the model outputs have fields or structures that break merge. The real failure is 'merge loses a real reviewer's assessment when processing actual LLM outputs.' This requires running two real reviewers to naturally generate conflicts, then verifying merge handles their actual output.
behaviors.test_description: Run the clause extractor and risk assessor on 10+ real contracts. Identify clauses where they produce conflicting assessments (extractor: 'standard term', risk assessor: 'high risk'). For each conflict, verify: (1) both assessments appear in the merged report without loss, (2) each assessment is correctly attributed to its source reviewer, (3) assessment content is preserved without modification, (4) no findings are dropped or duplicated. If natural conflicts are rare, include contracts with ambiguous language or policy edge cases to trigger reviewer disagreement.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires orchestrating three independent agentic components running in parallel, collecting their results, and merging them. Real failure modes include: (1) one reviewer's output is lost or not collected (race condition, timeout, collection bug), (2) merge silently discards findings from one reviewer, (3) parallel execution causes resource exhaustion or ordering issues. These can only occur when all three reviewers run their natural agentic loops in sequence/parallel. Testing requires full pipeline execution with all three real reviewers.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline end-to-end on 10+ real contracts. For each contract: (1) dispatch clause extractor, risk assessor, and compliance checker in parallel, (2) collect results from all three within timeout, (3) merge into unified report. Verify: (1) report contains findings from all three reviewers (none lost), (2) each reviewer's findings are correctly attributed and preserved, (3) no findings are duplicated, modified, or omitted, (4) pipeline completes within expected latency and resource constraints, (5) concurrent execution doesn't cause missed outputs or ordering issues. Validate final report against ground truth to ensure all three review perspectives are present and accurate.
